content.problemStatement:
For a sequence of positive integers A1,A2,…,AKA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_KA1​,A2​,…,AK​, let's define the number of inversions in it as the number of pairs of integers (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i<j≤K1 \le i \lt j \le K1≤i<j≤K and Ai>AjA_i \gt A_jAi​>Aj​.

You are given a permutation P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ of the integers 111 through NNN. Determine if it is possible to partition this permutation into two subsequences (possibly empty or non-contiguous) such that:


Each element of PPP appears in exactly one of these subsequences.
The numbers of inversions in one subsequence is equal to the number of inversions in the other subsequence.
constraints: 1≤Pi≤N1 \le P_i \le N1≤Pi​≤N for each valid iii
P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ are pairwise distinct
the sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 200200200
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing the string `"YES"` if it is possible to partition the permutation in a given way or `"NO"` if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: We can split (1)(1)(1) into (1)(1)(1) and ()()(). There are 000 inversions in each of these sequences.
Example case 3: We can split (4,3,2,1)(4, 3, 2, 1)(4,3,2,1) into (4,3)(4, 3)(4,3) and (2,1)(2, 1)(2,1). There is 111 inversion in each of them. Note that this is not the only solution ? we could also split the permutation into sequences (4,1)(4, 1)(4,1) and (3,2)(3, 2)(3,2).
